sentences.text: My father's name was Usher, Usher Honigman, and my mother's name was Gifa.
Her maid's name was Zilberstein.
My sister's name was-- one, the older one was Rifka, and my middle one was Dvoira.
We were 82 in the family.
They were all  slaughtered, like I mentioned in the number one.
And we were very happy.
My father was very happily married, [NON-ENGLISH].
My mother-- my father met my mother when he used to go to make wheat flour.
My mother lost her father in Kiev when she was a young girl, probably around 18, and she became a maid for one of her relatives, that he owned a mill.
My father came up with horse and wagon, and he saw a beautiful girl washing the floor.
And he talked to her in Yiddish, which they used to do.
She says, I've got to earn a living.
I lost my father.
I'm named after your father, Shuelik.
With one eye-- he lost one of his eyes-- and a big beard.
And my father says, to her, [NON-ENGLISH], you look so beautiful.
I mean, I can't offer you a better life, but I'll try my best.
I'm a hard worker, and I'm staying with my mother, with my [NON-ENGLISH].
And why don't you come with the horse?
Sit down there and come with me and have a look at it.
She says, yes, I'm going to take you up on it.
She got dressed, whatever she had.
She went out with the horse and wagon.
And it took them two days-- a good two days.
They didn't ride at night.
With the horse and wagon, they came there.
My mother, probably she was very happy.
I don't know if it was love or anything like that.
And they went and they got married.
And in Europe, and you're Jewish, when you have wedding, was they invited the whole city almost.
You knocked on the table and [NON-ENGLISH] and sat, it wasn't such a thing like invitation and things like that.
And everybody prepared something like a-- like a borscht, or a kugel, or whatever, and they brought it over to the [NON-ENGLISH], and that's the way.
They were dancing together.
Their wedding was probably going on for two, three days.
Everybody chipped in something.
And I wish my children would be happy.
And they were happy.
I never saw them have any arguments.
And I never saw them anything.
And they were all happy.
She was working end by end.
And that was the life, and that's the way my father and my mother-- they were always happy.
It was-- it was approximately around 4,000 people in that community, was 3,000 Jews.
Right, no, and it approximately was about, I don't say exactly, probably about 50 or 60 Polish people, and the rest was Ukrainian.
And we had a [NON-ENGLISH].
We had two Jewish schools, whoever could afford paying.
Unfortunately, I used to go to cheder, because we could not afford paying the tuition for the Jewish-- so I used to go to Ukrainian.
They used to teach you Ukrainian and Polish, no Jewish.
And they used to call me, sing a song every morning.
They used to call me [NON-ENGLISH].
That means the Jew is dirty, a song.
And I always used to walk around with stones in my pocket.
I used to throw them back.
They always used to come to complain to my mother that they used to go to the teacher saying that my-- that your son is a bad boy, throws stones and makes wounded children.
So my mother, the teacher said that these are minorities, the only one Jew-- I mean, I was in that class, the only one.
And, of course, my two sisters were higher grades.
But we were the only one Jews, so I had to protect myself, because I couldn't take it that they used to call me names.
And how old were you when that was happening?
At that time, when-- when I was old, this probably happened when I was 11 years old.
If you see it in that piece of paper what I showed it to you, it came out Ukrainian people, when I came into my house, that it's still standing there.
They fixed it up, and I start crying.
And the men didn't want to let me in.
And the roommates, it actually says, [NON-ENGLISH].
That's what it says right there, the advertising [NON-ENGLISH].
That means gut, gut.
That's you, sir Shmuelik.
I said, yes.
And they start hugging me, kissing me.
And one lady says, don't you remember me?
And she started pulling up the sleeve.
And she says to me, Shmuelik, you-- in Ukrainian-- you remember that you used to pinch me?
We used to sit next to each other.
You used to pinch me that I still have the bruises.
You don't remember?
And they start crying.
These-- these people walked with me, end by-- feet by feet, that's what it says right there.
To the grave, we walked two miles.
That was the order for me.
Two miles, the same steps, the same thing, we stopped even when the ditches where they threw the dead.
That was my wish-- to the grave.
These Ukrainian people, they walk with bouquets of flowers.
They were crying more than me.
They kissed the ground.
I wouldn't call these people they were killers, because these people, they walked more slow than they were my age.
Maybe their parents-- it was some of the elderly people walking too.
I wouldn't swear for them.
But these people were walking up to today.
And then later on, they start screaming to the man, they should let me into that house to look because my grandfather was born.
And they screamed that, give him back the house!
It's his house!
And the man got sterilized.
First, he didn't want let me in.
He let me in and he started shaking, an elderly man.
And I told him I said, I not-- didn't come to take away the house.
I came around to see.
And when I looked in that garden, what my mother had me-- we didn't have such a thing like a hospital.
My mother used to tell me many times for a joke, when I didn't listen to her, she says, Shmuelik, I should have left you in that-- that garden.
Why would I pick you up?
I had you there.
When I looked up in that, when I looked up at that beautiful trees, [SOBBING] that beautiful apple wood, I grew up there.
It broke my heart.
I took a taxi with my two children, who went there.
And first of all, I was thinking to take a policeman.
And I said to my children, no, I am not taking a policeman.
They will not touch me, because we were poor, the farmers.
They were all asking me to come to sleep there.
Up till today, they're writing me letters, the Ukrainians.
And-- and I phone them.
It takes a long time to phone.
And they are you know, they are so-- I don't know if they mean it at the heart-- very warm, very, very warm.
And so the-- after that, I spent almost four hours near that house till the taxi driver said, you're paying?
I said, yeah.
So between the taxi diver and my son, [SOBBING] they almost had to carry me out of that house.
I didn't want to go away.
I said, I want to stay here.
I feel good.
And up till today, it's very hard.
I'd like to go back to, again, when you were a young boy.
You said that you went to a school that-- that was not a Jewish school, and yet you lived in a town that was majority of Jewish population.
Right.
Yes but they didn't kill.
It was still against the law, killing.
The Polish people and the Ukrainian people, they were-- but again, they didn't punish much.
If they killed a Jew, they still didn't give him too much jail.
They used to give him a little jail.
They let him out.
But they still didn't-- it was accidents, looks like kill, but there was no law to kill Jews.
Very much.
My uncle, the one they took between the 200 people, I will never forget it as a child.
I remember very well because I was pretty mature.
I had-- my circumstances brought me to there, that I had to be mature.
I had a sick father.
I had to work, help him.
He took out, a couple times-- he used to be so scared-- of like a two-by-four piece of wood.
And he was fighting with 20-- over 20 Ukrainian people.
They were scared, very, very-- they were sticking out very, very-- there was a lot of Jewish people.
But the Ukrainian people used to be very, very scared for them.
We were not allowed to have any guns.
But they were good fighters.
They were sticking up very much, yes.
I would say so.
There was no guns, even for Poland, no.
It wasn't.
The Ukrainian people had no reason to-- no have guns or anything like that either.
No, it what was not allowed.
Only the police were allowed, and not for the Russian either.
No, no.
No, no, no.
It was equal.
No, not for the Russians, and not for the Jew-- Polish, no.
No, no, no.
The only one who made the tragedy is the Germans.
I don't remember that.
This was like an anti-Semitism, definitely, even with school.
They-- they used to bail out the child that discriminated against me.
But they didn't really kill it.
Used to call the parents.
They used to call-- but no, we didn't feel it, no.
Yeah, we knew-- we hear that this happened in 1939 in Germany.
We didn't believe it.
Just probably like you didn't believe it in the States what's going on.
We-- we should've believed, because that time started the killing already, some of it.
I wish we would have left.
So a lot of people, the Russians, send away to Siberia.
A lot of Jews, what they were-- for Israel, all they will reach, they send them to Siberia.
Then later on, people were crying for them.
In the meantime, these people, a lot of them, are alive.
You know, the other way.
I know a few families in Israel.
That's the one, some of the 18 people, they came from there.
They came from Siberia.
They were all there.
That's all the 18 people, most of them came from there.
There's two other ones where they were survive.
Like one of my friend, one, he had a big speech.
If-- If I will send you, these, I think it was six pages, that speech, the way, word by word, that I-- I dictated him.
And he had a speech-- because it broke my heart.
He had a speech by the grave site, right, when we were there, in three languages-- in English, and in Ukrainian, and in Hebrew, because it was there.

It would break my heart, like I told-- mentioned to you on the phone, when I went to translating it, that wording that he said it in these three languages, in English, I went to three ladies to type it out for me.
They were crying.
Finally, the fourth lady-- I could send you the letters, if you want to-- said she wants $20 for an hour to type it up for me.
I said, with pleasure.
I took out $200, I put it on the table.
I said here's the $200.
Whatever it will cost, I'll gladly pay you  for it.
After she was-- stopped typing a half a page, she cried, and she said, Sam, my heart doesn't give me.
So the husband came up.
He says, Sam went to three ladies.
Please type it.
After she typed it, she started crying hardly, very much.
And she said, Sam, I don't want your $200.
And we became friends up till today, you know.
Special, I'll tell you one incident there.
There was two little girls by the grave.
And I saw it with my own eyes, slowly digging out.
They looked so cute in their beautiful clothes.
And they announced it in Ukrainian, whoever wants the clothes, they have to throw in the two [NON-ENGLISH], the two little girls, in the grave alive.
One son-of-a-gun woman came up.
She took out the clothes.
She took them by the feet and threw them in-- in that thing.
You could imagine my heart saw it.
My eyes saw it.
My heart heard the screaming of that.
That's why I know things-- God, I'm-- I don't think so mentally.
But many nights [SOBBING] I hear the screams of these people.
I can't get it over.
It's already 45, 50 years.
These screams are there, and I'll never-- I'll die with those screams probably.
I can't help it.
They are there, my ears.
Doesn't matter what I do.
I'm well off.
It doesn't help me anything, but they're there.
I can't get rid of them.
Yes, it was close, because I knew the area, and I knew, more or less, because my father used to work for a lot of these farmers.
And I knew more or less some of them, they would be the killers.
For the example, we had one man that I was there myself.
I put in a head stone over him and over his nephew, a Baptist.
I have his pictures all over.
He helped me and my friend so much that probably I wouldn't be alive if it's not for him and with food.
I froze my feet.
I went into to him at nighttime, and I said to him, Nikolai, I came here.
You should kill me.
You are a religious man; kill me.
I want to die there.
And I told them, I lied, that I wanted to become a Baptist.
He digged out the grave, like a dig.
In the top of house was going two pigs and a cow.
And he kept me there almost three weeks, me and my friend in that thing.
He cured my feet with pig fat.
And he risked his life.
So when I came [INAUDIBLE], some Ukrainian told me that that man lost his nephew.
He was never be married.
His name was Mykolai Zozulia.
His brother's son, they took the Ukrainian people.

They tied them up with a string, and they pulled all over, and they put up a sign that that's-- he's going to die slowly, dragging him, because he saved the two Jews for witnesses, Shmuelik and Monek Averbuch.
That man, his brother never knew even that.
And his nephew never knew that.
He was unguilty.
But what happened?
He made a mistake.
After we were liberated, that-- his brother and his son became very friendly.
The Ukrainian people used to watch him coming into us.
We took away a house-- Jew, one man in the city, and we give it away to his brother.
And he thought, that's the one.
They went away at nighttime.
They took the man, and that's the way that they perished.
His brother died.
And that man, what he saved-- what he helped us, giving the food, they told me that he has no head stone.
I should leave so, my son will say that.
I went away to a contractor, and I give them an order that I want to have two head stones in Ukrainian cemetery, one for his-- the one that was slaughtered, that they killed, and one of him.
And they're giving me a big blessing.
Up till today, the community wrote me a thank-you letter.
That was a good one.
It cost me $375 for each one.
That was the best investment.
Plus I give his wife-- I'm not trying to brag.
I give his wife $500 American money, and my friend give $500.
They-- that's a lot of money to them.
I give his children some money.
And up till today, I'll send him some money that I never knew them.
But the wife lost your husband before that come to pass, unguilty.
Very fine people-- they give me some nice souvenirs.
And she calls me, the time, like in the family there, Ukrainian.
And they had offered me-- and-- and one of his sons, by the way, is now a policeman in the police in Ukraine.
When we went to the grave to put on that stone, the policeman went in civilian clothes and he puts in a gun in his pocket.
So my friend in Israel says to him, listen, you have an extra gun?
Because in case they start shooting, I'll help you.
I know-- I-- I fought a lot in Israel.
I know that.
So he was holding the gun right in his pocket.
He says, Shmuelik?
Anybody will try to embarrass you, I wouldn't look for law.
I'll shoot him right there on the grave.
And he was very nice about it.
You know, I don't know if he meant it, but that's the way he was talking.
I'd like to go back to the time during the war.
How did you and your friend know that it was safe to try to go from farmer to farmer, or to go into the woods?
How--
Never safe.
You had to take the chances, and you had-- you had to be so fast.
I'll tell you a little incident-- I think I told you on the phone-- that I'll never forget it.
I had that time before I went into that Ukraine with my frozen feet.
And I went away and I couldn't stand up.
My feet was frozen and swollen up, you know, all the way.
And we heard they were shooting in the forest, they were looking for-- it was 1944, end of 1944 almost.
And not Jews-- they were looking for partisans.
The Ukrainian police and the Germans were scared to go into the forest, because they didn't know the forest.
It might be grenades, it might blow them up.
They were shooting-- people should come out.
I heard shooting.
I set to Manek, Manek-- in Yiddish-- [YIDDISH].
Then I, of course, I spoke to them in Ukrainian.
I knew a little bit, because I didn't know that-- I didn't want people should hear Jewish language.
So Manek says, how could I run, you calling?
So he took me in the-- in the hand.
I was weighing probably at that time 80, 90 pounds, like a skeleton.
He was always healthy.
He-- he started crying.
He said, Shmuelik, you run for the grave, we run before the grave.
[NON-ENGLISH].
I'm going to die right here with you.
I will not leave you alone.
He took-- and that's the time I directed him and we went to that Baptist, and he saved me my feet that time.
I told him which way to go.
He walked almost the whole night, dragging me.
We went, and I had no other choice.
Otherwise, I would be dead.
And I figured, let's take a chance.
Here, I'm giving you the instruction what you have to take chances.
You have to be very fast.
Is far but food, up till today.
When we used to have a piece of bread-- and I swear with my life-- my Manek used to chew the piece of bread, the same piece of bread.
Before swallowing the piece of bread-- many times it happened-- he used to take out the piece of bread and put it in there.
He said, Shmuelik, [SOBBING] you need it more than me.
You can't walk.
He didn't want to swallow that, he used to give it to me.
That's the answer.
That's why he's crying right now in Israel.
But he memorized all that stuff that we're talking about it.
Why?
Because the shooting was there.
Heavy shooting, very heavy shooting.
They were fighting every bridges, they were fighting in our city by the water.
And when it stopped-- the shooting-- and we were still there, we knew they were pushed back.
They didn't give up, the Germans.
The Rovno was smashed, about 80% of the buildings.
Rovno has got 300,000 Jews-- that's 40 miles from Mezhyrich.
They were fighting there, I would say about two weeks, that's the time.
And they were pushed back.
No-- even when I was-- after that, and then city after city, they would kill-- I mean, they lost a lot of people.
Millions-- I think it was 18 million people totally, not just the Jews-- was 6 million-- but they lost another 12 million, between the Russians and the-- you know, the Ukrainians, altogether.
Not talking about deaths.
We saw ourselves, in the forest, so many dead people.
Heads, feet.
It's hard to believe it.
Not just Jews-- they used to kill each other.
They used to have three kinds of partisans, the Ukrainians.
They used to be [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], and [NON-ENGLISH].
That was all Ukrainian.
The [NON-ENGLISH] said like that, that the Jews could be alive.
Germans could be alive, but they have to be slaves.
The [NON-ENGLISH] said like the Germans, .
No nation should be alive.
They should have some state of Ukraine.
That's what they're having right now.
The-- the [NON-ENGLISH] said, yes, the Jews could be alive, and they were good, and we've got to save them.
So there used to be many times two brothers-- they were two different parties-- they used to kill each other.
How did they kill them?
With knives, whatever they had.
You know, that was a lot of-- that was going on, we didn't know who to make-- you had to be very, very fast-- fast-thinking-- because you never know.
Every second, the bullet goes so fast, you'll be killed.
And if you don't take chances, you can't be survive, because how will you steal food?
How will you-- I mean, summertime was a cinch because we had a lot of food there growing, and there used to be the heads for the cabbage that we used to pick them up and chew them.
We used to eat, many times, from that dead horse.
We used to come around-- anything which was biting, what you eat.
You try to do it.
Water, we used to drink from little ditches, wherever it was a little water to get.
Dirt-- we were full, pardon my expression, with lice.
Lice was all over.
I used to take in all over.
We were very filthy.
But God saved me from that.
I didn't have [NON-ENGLISH].
That's why [SOBBING] I believe that must be somebody miracle happened.
I was-- I wasn't made smart.
If I would be smart, maybe I would-- maybe I should have gone with the parents to go to the grave.
So probably, I was mean that way.
I was selfish.
I still say I don't deserve anything, but probably somebody wants I should do it.
I was right there in the forest, right there.
We walked into our small little town--
Mezhyrich.
Mezhyrich.
And we took away a big house, and we were probably approximately just about 30 partisans.
And we were standing there-- we were sleeping right on the floor together with the partisans.
I was a child at the time.
I was 15.
I didn't feel any discriminating.
They knew that I am Jewish.
They used to call me [NON-ENGLISH], that means son.
And when they had the food, they had the food.
They would not share the food, because they used to go into the farmers-- they didn't give them.
They used to kill them.
But they used to give me the best food, because they wanted to bring me-- I should be healthy.
I didn't feel-- they were very nice to me, but you had to obey it.
A lot of the children, they had to go to the-- to the-- you know, in the ditches to put away a pail, and it used to blow up many times.
Fortunately, I was too young.
They used to send me with  the horse-- with the horse and the cows for the pasture there, to give them food.
What happened-- they did not go ahead, and it never blowed up when they sent me there.
They probably-- they didn't want to hurt me.
They saw that I'm weak, and they didn't-- you know, they watched me.
They should be there.
I didn't wear any ammunition.
My friend, yes.
He was wearing a gun and he was blowing up a lot of stuff, and I would say-- I didn't see it, but he killed a lot of-- a lot of people.
Yes?
Yes.
No, no.
I would say most-- if all this Holocaust-- what they were there in the front and knew whatever it was all over the world-- If it's not the Russians, would be very few Holocausts left.
The only one liberated was-- is the Russians.
I'm not a Communist, but I have salute to the Russians because we wouldn't be alive.
Right, right.
I was taken to hospital in Rovno.
When it was taken away, the Rovno, the first day they were bumping because we didn't have no doctors there.
They took me and they kept me in the hospital almost six weeks, and they brought me shape.
And after that, I was three months back in my small town, but they took away our house and they give it to me next to the police station.
And they were watching me like a rock.
Me, my friend was in the police force.
They were watching me like a rock.
After that, I saw sooner or later Ukrainians were going to kill me.
Because after they hanged up the 20 people, they saw that-- that we were the witnesses.
In Mezhyrich. 20 police, they killed.
Right.
The first day when they came in, the second day, they hanged them.

Because they figured that-- the-- the partisans figured themselves that there will be themselves in trouble, because maybe they still have the guns, or maybe they will come around at night time and fight with them.
So they picked up the ones where the-- anybody squawked, and they tried to kill-- and they killed them.
They didn't-- there was not such a thing like a trial.
They have to save their own life too, because after all, they know how to fight, and they were-- they know they are guilty.
They were the killers for the Jews.
And sooner or later, they going to be taken.
